audit_status.source_base: Greek 1 Corinthians text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for marriage, mutual conjugal authority, singleness gift, mixed marriage, call/condition, present distress, undivided devotion. Preserve pastoral counsel, not universal coercion.

verses.restored: Now concerning the things you wrote: It is beautiful/good for a human not to touch a woman.
But because of sexual immoralities/misuses, let each man have his own wife, and each woman have her own husband.
Let the husband give to the wife what is owed, and likewise also the wife to the husband.
The wife does not have authority over her own body, but the husband; likewise also the husband does not have authority over his own body, but the wife.

Do not deprive one another, except perhaps by agreement for a season, so that you may devote yourselves to prayer, and then be together again, so that the adversarial one may not test you because of your lack of self-control.
But I say this as concession, not as command.
I wish all humans were as I myself am; but each has one's own gift from the Divine, one this way and another that way.
To the unmarried and widows I say: it is beautiful/good for them if they remain as I also am.
But if they do not have self-control, let them marry; for it is better to marry than to burn.
To the married I command, not I but the Lord: a wife is not to be separated from a husband,
but if she is separated, let her remain unmarried or be reconciled to the husband; and a husband is not to send away a wife.
But to the rest I say, I, not the Lord: if any sibling has an untrusting wife and she consents to dwell with him, let him not send her away.
And if any woman has an untrusting husband and he consents to dwell with her, let her not send away the husband.
For the untrusting husband is sanctified in the wife, and the untrusting wife is sanctified in the sibling; otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy.
But if the untrusting one separates, let that one separate. The sibling, man or woman, has not been enslaved in such cases; in peace the Divine has called us.
For how do you know, wife, whether you will save/heal the husband? Or how do you know, husband, whether you will save/heal the wife?
Only, as the Lord assigned to each, as the Divine has called each, so let one walk. And thus I direct in all the assemblies.
Was anyone called having been circumcised? Let him not draw over. Has anyone been called in uncircumcision? Let him not be circumcised.
Circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing, but keeping the commandments of the Divine.
Let each remain in the calling in which one was called.
Were you called as enslaved? Do not let it trouble you; but if you are able to become free, rather use it.
For the one called in the Lord as enslaved is the Lord's freed one; likewise the one called as free is Messiah's enslaved one.
You were bought with a price; do not become enslaved to humans.
Siblings, in what each was called, let each remain with the Divine.
Now concerning virgins/unmarried ones, I have no command of the Lord, but I give judgment as one shown mercy by the Lord to be trustworthy.
I think therefore this is beautiful/good because of the present necessity/distress: that it is beautiful/good for a human to be thus.
Have you been bound to a wife? Do not seek release. Have you been released from a wife? Do not seek a wife.
But if you marry, you did not sin/misalign; and if the virgin/unmarried one marries, she did not sin/misalign. Yet such will have pressure in the flesh, and I am sparing you.
This I say, siblings: the season has been shortened; from now on, let those having wives be as though not having,
and those weeping as though not weeping, those rejoicing as though not rejoicing, those buying as though not possessing,
and those using the world as not using it fully; for the form of this world is passing away.
I want you to be without anxiety. The unmarried man is anxious for the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord;
but the married man is anxious for the things of the world, how he may please the wife,

and he is divided. The unmarried woman and the virgin are anxious for the things of the Lord, to be holy in body and spirit; but the married woman is anxious for the things of the world, how she may please the husband.
I say this for your own benefit, not so that I may throw a noose upon you, but toward what is well-formed and devoted to the Lord without distraction.
But if anyone thinks he is acting improperly toward his virgin/unmarried one, if she is beyond prime and so it ought to happen, let him do what he wills; he does not sin/misalign; let them marry.

But the one who stands firm in his heart, having no necessity but having authority concerning his own will, and has judged this in his own heart to keep his virgin/unmarried one, will do beautifully/well.
So then the one marrying his virgin/unmarried one does beautifully/well, and the one not marrying will do better.
A wife is bound for as long a time as her husband lives; but if the husband sleeps, she is free to be married to whom she wills, only in the Lord.
But she is more blessed/flourishing if she remains thus, according to my judgment; and I think I also have Spirit of the Divine.

translation_notes: Romans-style care continues: this chapter is pastoral counsel under present distress, not universal coercion.
Verses 36-38 are syntactically difficult and may refer to a betrothed daughter/ward or one's own betrothed; restored wording preserves ambiguity with virgin/unmarried one.
